accepted.question_text: Given a continuous graph Gamma=(V,E) and a radius r>0, does there always exist a finite predetermined discretization of points of Gamma, such as endpoints together with finitely many subdividing points on edges, that preserves the r-independence number alpha_r(Gamma) by guaranteeing that some maximum r-independent set can be chosen entirely from that finite point set?

evidence.quote: Discretization and tractability. Is there a discretization of the edge set of a continuous graph preserving the r-independence number?
This question was addressed and solved by Hartmann and Lendl [14] and Hartmann [12].

accepted.question_text: For the maximum r-independent set problem on a continuous graph, does every yes-instance admit a polynomial-size certificate, equivalently a solution whose relevant point locations can be encoded with polynomially bounded rational data?

evidence.quote: Moreover, they show that numerators and denominators of these rational distances are polynomially bounded in the input of the problem, which answers yet another non-trivial complexity question: Is there a polynomial size certificate (read polynomially encoded solution) for the maximum r-independent set problem in general continuous graphs?
Here, the answer is yes, such a certificate does exist, based on the aforementioned rational distances.

accepted.question_text: For the maximum r-independent set problem on a continuous graph Gamma, how does computational complexity depend jointly on the radius r and on the structure of the underlying graph, beyond the known cases where some small radii and sufficiently large radii are polynomial-time solvable while many other rational radii are NP-hard?

evidence.quote: Now, when we know that the problem is in NP, the question is: For which values of r the problem is solvable in polynomial time and for which values it becomes NP-hard?
Thus, the open question of this section is to have a picture of such a joint dependency.

accepted.question_text: Let G be a combinatorial graph, let alpha(G) denote the size of a maximum independent set of G, and let alpha_1(Gamma(G)) denote the largest size of a 1-independent set in its continuous counterpart Gamma(G), where points of the continuous edges may be chosen as set members and any two chosen points must be at distance at least 2. Is n/2 a tight universal upper bound on the ratio alpha_1(Gamma(G))/alpha(G), and how does this ratio behave on special graph classes?

evidence.quote: It is unclear whether or not n/2 is a tight upper bound for the ratio alpha1(Gamma(G))/alpha(G). We leave this as an open question of this section.
Furthermore, we do expect that some structural properties of combinatorial graphs influence the ratio, and it is interesting to see how the ratio behaves in special cases/classes of graphs.

accepted.question_text: For every rational radius r>0, does there exist a polynomial-time approximation algorithm for the maximum r-independent set problem on continuous graphs whose approximation ratio is bounded below by an absolute constant independent of the input graph?

evidence.quote: The specific open question we ask in this paragraph reads: For any rational number r > 0, is there a polynomial time approximation algorithm for the maximum r-independent set problem with performance guarantee bounded by a constant?

accepted.question_text: Let alpha_r(Gamma) be the maximum size of a set of points in a continuous graph Gamma whose pairwise distances are at least 2r, and let beta_r(Gamma) be the minimum number of radius-r balls whose union covers all points of Gamma. For a continuous graph with n endpoints, what bound holds for alpha_r(Gamma)+beta_r(Gamma) as a function of n and r, and is that bound tight?

evidence.quote: In general, the following question can be addressed: Given r > 0, what is the bound on alpha_r(Gamma) + beta_r(Gamma) for a continuous graph Gamma in terms of n and r? Is the bound tight?

accepted.question_text: For a combinatorial graph G and its continuous counterpart Gamma(G), let beta(G) be the minimum vertex-cover size in G and let beta_1(Gamma(G)) be the minimum number of radius-1 balls centered anywhere on Gamma(G) that cover all points of the continuous graph. What is the largest possible ratio beta(G)/beta_1(Gamma(G)) over all graphs G; in particular, is this ratio always at most 2?

evidence.quote: Similarly to the independent set problem above, the following natural open question arises: What is the highest ratio between beta(G) and beta1(Gamma(G)) over all G?
Following intuition from the matching theory and known results for the minimum vertex cover problem, we conjecture the ratio is at most 2.

accepted.question_text: For the hard instances of the minimum r-cover problem on continuous graphs, namely when r is a non-unit fraction, what lower bounds on approximation hardness can be proved for computing the minimum number of radius-r balls needed to cover the graph?

evidence.quote: We suggest the following straightforward open question: For the hard cases of the problem (with the radius being a non-unit fraction, see Hartmann et al [16]), what are the lower bounds for the inapproximability of the minimum r-cover problem?

accepted.question_text: Let K_n be the complete graph on n vertices and let Gamma(K_n) be its continuous counterpart in which each edge is a unit-length interval. If chi_{1/2}(Gamma(K_n)) denotes the minimum number of colors needed for a cover of Gamma(K_n) by radius-1/2 balls such that intersecting balls receive different colors, what is the exact value of chi_{1/2}(Gamma(K_n))?

evidence.quote: A seemingly simple question to ask is: What is the 1/2-chromatic number of Gamma(K_n)?
We conjecture that the 1/2-chromatic number of Gamma(K_n) is ceiling(n/2).

accepted.question_text: Is every continuous planar graph (1/2,3)-colourable, meaning that all points of the graph can be covered by radius-1/2 balls that can be colored with at most three colors so that any two intersecting balls receive different colors? Equivalently, can one prove that three colors always suffice, or exhibit a continuous planar graph that forces at least three colors and certify that lower bound?

evidence.quote: We conjecture that every continuous planar graph is (1/2, 3)-colourable.
To get better intuition for this question, it would be helpful to find a continuous planar graph requiring at least 3 colours and to provide a formal proof of this lower bound of 3.

accepted.question_text: Does the severe inapproximability known for the chromatic number of combinatorial graphs persist for the 1/2-chromatic number of continuous graphs, or does allowing radius-1/2 balls on continuous edges make the problem asymptotically easier to approximate?

evidence.quote: The question for the continuous version of the chromatic number problem remains as above: Whether or not the same hardness of approximation remains for the 1/2-chromatic number problem on continuous graphs?

accepted.question_text: Given a continuous graph Gamma, can one derive informative lower and upper bounds for chi_{1/2}(Gamma), the minimum number of colors in a valid radius-1/2 ball cover, as a function of the chromatic number chi(Gamma's combinatorial counterpart) and the number n of endpoints, or as a function of other polynomial-time computable graph parameters?

evidence.quote: an interesting question to address is: Given a continuous graph Gamma, can one specify a lower and an upper bounds for chi1/2(Gamma) in terms of a function of chi(G(Gamma)) and n?
Any other bounds for chi1/2 as functions of other (especially, polynomial-time computable) parameters are also very much interesting.

accepted.question_text: Let the 1-bramble number of a continuous graph Gamma(G) be defined as the maximum order of a family of subtrees of Gamma(G) that pairwise come within distance 1, where the order is the minimum number of points needed to hit every subtree in the family. For a fixed small integer k>0, is there a tractable algorithm to decide whether the 1-bramble number of Gamma(G) is at most k?

evidence.quote: recognizing whether the 1-bramble number of a continuous graph is at most k, for a given small integer k > 0, might be a very challenging and not necessarily tractable problem. We leave this as an open problem of this section.

accepted.question_text: For the minimum r-cover problem on continuous graphs, where one must cover every point of the graph by radius-r balls centered anywhere on the graph, what is the complexity when r is an algebraic real number? More specifically, if r is algebraic and not a unit fraction, must the problem be NP-hard?

evidence.quote: As an interesting open problem, Hartmann et al. [16] suggested to study the complexity of the problem with an algebraic real r.
Recently, Hartmann and Lendl [14] proved the conjecture for both cover and independent set problems even for all real r > 0 that are not unit fractions.

trace.notes: Mapped the paper as a survey with Preliminaries followed by Open Problems split into Maximum independent set, Vertex cover, Chromatic number, and Treewidth. Core notation recovered from Definitions 9, 10, 11, and 12-13. Likely open-problem regions are pages 4-11 under Section 3.
Extracted five maximum-independent-set candidates, including two explicitly marked as solved and three open questions on complexity, relaxation gap, and approximability.
Extracted four vertex-cover candidates: alpha-beta duality, algebraic-radii complexity, relaxation gap, and inapproximability.
Extracted four chromatic-number candidates covering complete graphs, planar graphs, approximation hardness, and parameter bounds.
Extracted one treewidth candidate on recognizing bounded 1-bramble number.
